text: Hello, I am Joseph Green and I will be presenting a case of robotic completion cholecystectomy after a prior subtotal reconstituting cholecystectomy. I have no disclosures. The objectives of this video presentation are to illustrate the use of indocyanine green fluorescence cholangiography for identification of biliary structures as well as the use of robotic surgery for technically challenging dissections. Additionally, I will review the importance of complete radiographic completion cholangiography.

The patient is a fifty-five-year-old woman who presented to my office as an outpatient after a recent admission for antibiotic management of acute calculus cholecystitis. She has a past medical history of arthritis and vitamin D deficiency, for which she takes vitamin D, and a past surgical history of a laparoscopic converted open cholecystectomy ten years prior. Her CAT scan shows a significantly thickened gallbladder wall, biliary ductal dilatation, and calculi. Her labs have normalized at this time. A review of her records from the index operation indicates that she had underwent a laparoscopic converted to open subtotal reconstituting cholecystectomy. In this technique, the gallbladder wall is sutured closed when inflammation at the angle of collo precludes attainment of the critical view of safety. This technique has a higher risk of recurrent cholecystitis, but a lower risk of biliary fistula than the alternative method of subtotal fenestrating cholecystectomy.

We offered her completion cholecystectomy for treatment of her recurrent bouts of cholecystitis with use of indesignine green to help us identify biliary structures during the difficult dissection. For review, ICG is a sulfur-containing fluorophore, which is responsive to near-infrared light, emitting a green color with peak spectral absorption around eight hundred nanometers. During the minutes after intravenous injection, it can be seen in the arterial circulation for use in verifying blood flow to a bowel anastomosis or tissue of questionable vascular supply. Within twenty to thirty minutes, it undergoes hepatic clearance and biliary excretion. Subsequently, there is a two to four hour window where it can be used to assist with the identification of bile-containing structures, including the gallbladder, bile ducts, and the duodenum.

The surgeon utilizes the dexterity of the robotic surgery platform to access the abdomen through the patient's prior incisions. An optical trocar technique is used for entry, followed by the placement of robotic ports. A brief laparoscopic lysis of adhesions is performed to create adequate working space before docking the robot.

Robotic instruments are inserted, and the surgeon performs extensive lysis of dense adhesions in the right upper quadrant to expose the liver and gallbladder. Sharp dissection is used to separate adhesions between the liver and duodenum, while the liver is lifted to remove adhesions from the omentum, with the hepatic flexure of the colon visible beneath.

The surgeon begins by taking down adhesions laterally, using a port hop maneuver to reposition the camera to arm three with two graspers on the left. The robotic camera's near-infrared light is utilized to enhance visualization of biliary structures with indocyanine green. The omentum is carefully dissected away from the exposed gallbladder plate, which was revealed by the previous cholecystectomy.

The gallbladder becomes visible, highlighted by indocyanine green fluorescence, which aids in identifying bile-containing structures such as the duodenum and biliary tree. A sharp dissection creates a plane between the gallbladder and duodenum. The gallbladder is elevated, providing exposure to the angle of Calot, where dissection begins laterally.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum along both sides of the gallbladder adjacent to the liver, employing the extended critical view of safety technique. Near-infrared light and indocyanine green are used to enhance the identification of the biliary ductal anatomy. The angle of Calot is developed.

The surgeon makes an incision along the cystic duct, identifying the anterior and posterior branches of the cystic artery. The absence of bile in these structures is confirmed using indocyanine green (ICG) imaging. The critical view of safety is obtained and verified from the lateral side using the doublet view technique with ICG. The cystic arteries are then transected to create space for a radiographic cholangiogram.

A clip is applied to the gallbladder, and a cholangiogram catheter is introduced. A ductotomy is performed, and the catheter is secured in place. The initial cholangiogram shows no filling of the hepatic ducts or evidence of choledocholithiasis.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the liver using a domed down technique, employing near-infrared light and indocyanine green (ICG) to maintain the dissection plane through dense inflammation. This approach is taken to better align the ductal anatomy for a subsequent cholangiogram.

The gallbladder is completely removed from the liver bed, and fluorescence cholangiography is used to confirm the ductal anatomy. A suture secures the cholangiogram catheter due to the short cystic duct. Morphine is administered to promote sphincter contraction, and the patient is placed in the Trendelenburg position for the subsequent cholangiogram, which shows complete filling of the right and left hepatic ducts and biliary radicals. The cholangiogram catheter is removed, and the cystic duct is ligated with the suture.

Two locking clips are placed on the side of the common bile duct using fluorescence cholangiography to ensure precise placement and avoid narrowing. The first clip is removed, and the gallbladder is placed into a specimen bag for removal from the abdomen.

The patient was discharged home on post-operative day one and is doing well at follow-up. In summary, the objectives of this presentation were to demonstrate the use of indesigning green fluorescence cholangiography for identification of biliary structures, as well as the use of the robotic surgery platform for technically challenging dissection. Our cholangiograms demonstrate the importance of complete radiographic completion cholangiography with retrograde hepatic ductal filling. Thank you very much for your attention and I will be happy to take any questions.
